title: Control vs. Amplification
subtitle: Information System Divergence in the U.S.–Israel–Iran Conflict: Evidence from China’s Signaling System

alternate_identifiers.description: Short form of the policy brief title

description: This policy brief develops an Amplification–Accumulation Divergence framework (AADF) to analyze information-system divergence in the U.S.–Israel–Iran conflict. Using China’s signaling system as the controlled-signaling case and external information environments as decentralized amplification systems, it argues that information instability is driven primarily by event amplification rather than by baseline narrative intensity.
abstract: The U.S.–Israel–Iran conflict has produced not only military escalation, but also a structural divergence in how information is generated, transmitted, and amplified across different systems. This policy brief proposes an Amplification–Accumulation Divergence framework (AADF) to explain how information systems generate path-dependent divergence under asymmetric amplification dynamics. The analysis examines China’s information signaling system through a computational framework based on a baseline–event dual-layer model, operationalized using the Signaling Pressure Index (SPI) and the Event Amplification Ratio (EAR). The brief finds that China maintains a stable baseline narrative with controlled release patterns, whereas external information systems are dominated by event-driven amplification spikes linked to battlefield developments, economic disruptions, and political signaling. Divergence arises less from narrative volume than from amplification dynamics, especially when event-driven amplification temporarily exceeds baseline absorption capacity.

series_information.series_name: EPINOVA Policy Brief Series on Strategic Competition, AI-Enabled Warfare, and Information Conflict
series_information.series_description: Policy brief series translating EPINOVA research on strategic competition, AI-enabled warfare, global security governance, and information conflict into concise policy-facing analysis.

methodology.source_basis: Policy-facing computational and conceptual analysis based on a baseline–event dual-layer information model operationalized through the Signaling Pressure Index (SPI) and Event Amplification Ratio (EAR).
methodology.case_context: Information-system divergence during the U.S.–Israel–Iran conflict, with China’s signaling system treated as a controlled-signaling regime and external information environments treated as decentralized amplification systems.
methodology.core_problem: Conventional narrative comparison does not adequately explain why information systems diverge during high-intensity conflict. The central problem is how baseline-controlled signaling systems and event-driven amplification systems process shocks differently and generate nonlinear divergence.
central_claims: China operates a baseline-controlled information signaling system characterized by stability, delayed responsiveness, and low amplification volatility.
External information environments function as decentralized, event-driven amplification systems that produce rapid and high-intensity signaling spikes.
Divergence between the two systems is structural rather than narrative, emerging from differences in information dynamics rather than content alone.
Information instability is driven primarily by event amplification rather than by variations in baseline narrative intensity.
The interaction between baseline control and event amplification produces a dual-layer signaling structure with nonlinear escalation patterns.
The central systemic risk lies not in narrative competition itself, but in the loss of amplification control during high-intensity events.

analytical_lenses.name: Baseline–Event Dual-Layer Model
analytical_lenses.description: Separates continuous baseline signaling from discrete event-driven amplification spikes in order to identify the source of information pressure.

analytical_lenses.name: Amplification–Accumulation Divergence
analytical_lenses.description: Explains divergence as a path-dependent process in which repeated amplification spikes exceed absorption capacity and create residual shifts across engagement cycles.

analytical_lenses.name: Control vs. Amplification System Logic
analytical_lenses.description: Contrasts controlled signaling regimes with decentralized amplification markets to explain differences in stability, responsiveness, volatility, and escalation risk.

analytical_lenses.name: Threshold and Absorption Capacity
analytical_lenses.description: Treats EAR greater than 1 as a potential threshold condition in which event-driven amplification exceeds baseline signaling capacity.

dimensions.name: Baseline Signaling
dimensions.description: Stable, continuous narrative production associated with official discourse, policy positioning, and long-cycle communication strategies.

dimensions.name: Event-Driven Amplification
dimensions.description: Short-term amplification spikes triggered by discrete developments such as military strikes, political statements, economic disruptions, and other high-intensity events.

dimensions.name: Signaling Pressure Index
dimensions.description: Measures total information intensity as the sum of baseline signaling and event-driven amplification: SPI = Baseline + Event.

dimensions.name: Event Amplification Ratio
dimensions.description: Captures the relative weight of event-driven spikes compared with baseline signaling: EAR = Event / Baseline. EAR greater than 1 indicates that amplification exceeds baseline signaling capacity.

dimensions.name: System Absorption Capacity
dimensions.description: Represents a threshold condition under which the system can absorb event-driven amplification without entering a divergence or regime-shift pattern.

key_findings: China operates a baseline-controlled information signaling system characterized by stability, delayed responsiveness, and low amplification volatility.
External information environments operate as decentralized, event-driven amplification systems that generate rapid and high-intensity signaling spikes.
Divergence between China’s signaling system and external information systems is structural rather than merely narrative.
Information instability is driven primarily by event amplification rather than by ordinary variation in baseline narrative intensity.
The baseline–event interaction produces a dual-layer signaling structure with nonlinear escalation patterns.
Maximum divergence occurs when amplification becomes disproportionate relative to baseline stability, not necessarily when narratives are strongest.
Repeated amplification cycles may produce path-dependent divergence when event amplification exceeds system absorption capacity and leaves residual shifts across cycles.
The central systemic risk lies in the loss of amplification control during high-intensity events.
policy_recommendations: Shift analysis of information competition from narrative-volume comparison toward amplification-dynamics assessment.
Monitor event-driven amplification indicators, including SPI and EAR, during high-intensity conflict events.
Assess whether amplification spikes exceed system absorption capacity and create threshold conditions for information divergence.
Differentiate between controlled signaling regimes and decentralized amplification markets when evaluating information escalation risks.
Treat amplification control, rather than narrative production alone, as a central variable in modern information competition.

figures.title: Two-Layer Information Structure: Main Narrative vs. Event Spikes
figures.description: Line chart comparing baseline narrative intensity with event-driven amplification signals for Feb 28–Mar 23, 2026, China; the solid line represents baseline narrative intensity and the dashed line represents event spikes.

figures.title: Amplification–Accumulation Divergence Mechanism
figures.description: Mechanism diagram showing how amplification spikes can exceed system absorption capacity, trigger divergence initiation, generate cumulative cost–risk divergence, and carry residual shifts into subsequent engagement cycles.

tables.title: Structural Comparison of Information Signaling Systems
tables.description: Compares China as a controlled signaling regime with external systems as decentralized amplification markets across dimensions including core structure, baseline stability, amplification volatility, event responsiveness, release pattern, coherence, drivers, objectives, triggers, and risk profile.
